unit: Poetry: Exploring imagery, form and structure
title: Making Your Dragon Tankas Even Better!
simple_explanation: We're going to add sparkle and shine to our dragon poems! We'll pick super-strong words to make pictures in people's heads. Also, we'll make our poems sound cool by using 'sound tricks' like repeating 's' sounds (s-s-s-s) or 'a' sounds (ah-ah-ah). Then, you get to be the poet and writer, making up your own dragon story poems and even performing them for your friends!
detailed_explanation: You'll learn to choose words carefully, perhaps using a thesaurus to find exciting new synonyms. This helps to create stronger 'imagery' – the mental pictures your poem creates. We'll also add special sound effects to our poems! These are called poetic devices like 'consonance' (repeating consonant sounds), 'assonance' (repeating vowel sounds), and 'sibilance' (lots of 's' sounds). They make your poem sound musical. Finally, you'll get to write your very own haikus and tankas about a dragon's adventure! Imagine what your dragon looks like and what amazing things it does. Then, you'll share your poems by performing them, using your voice and actions to bring them to life.

examples_expanded.text: Instead of just 'sad,' you might use a thesaurus to find 'melancholy' or 'woeful' for stronger imagery.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of refining poetry with poetic devices in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: For consonance, 'dark, deep dread' repeats the 'd' sound. For assonance, 'the light shines bright' repeats the 'i' sound.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of refining poetry with poetic devices in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: For sibilance, 'slithering snakes silently sleep' creates a hissing sound, just like a snake!
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of refining poetry with poetic devices in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'thesaurus' in a sentence. Remember it means: a book or online tool that lists words with similar meanings (synonyms) and opposite meanings (antonyms).
Match the word 'imagery' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Refining Poetry with Poetic Devices.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'thesaurus' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: Good vocabulary choices improve the 'imagery' of a poem.
Poetic devices like consonance, assonance, and sibilance add musicality and impact to your writing.
Planning a story helps when writing longer poems like tankas.
Performing your poems helps you understand how sound and expression affect the audience.

vocabulary.definition: a book or online tool that lists words with similar meanings (synonyms) and opposite meanings (antonyms).
the use of descriptive words to create vivid pictures or sensations in the reader's mind.
the repetition of consonant sounds (like 't' or 'd') in nearby words, often at the end of words or in the middle.
the repetition of vowel sounds (like 'a' or 'o') in nearby words.
the repetition of 's' sounds, often creating a hissing or whispering effect.
the repetition of the same letter or sound at the beginning of words in a sentence, like 'bouncing baby boy'.
